items.text: Blessed be God, at the end of the last year I was in very good health, without any sense of my old pain but upon taking of cold.
items.context: Opening entry of Pepys' diary, reflecting on his personal health and broader state during the Restoration period following Cromwell's death
items.publicReaction: Not publicly known until diary's publication in 1825; modern scholars note it as characteristically detailed and personal observation
items.source: The Diary of Samuel Pepys (1660-1669), published and edited by various scholars including Robert Latham and William Matthews

items.text: The condition of the State was thus. Viz. the Rump, after being disturbed by my Lord Lambert, was lately returned to sit again.
items.context: Early January 1660 entry documenting the political turmoil following Cromwell's death and the instability of the Rump Parliament
items.publicReaction: Not public during his lifetime; provides historians with contemporary political observations from an official in the transition to the Restoration

items.text: I have newly taken a solemn oath about abstaining from plays and wine…
items.context: New Year's Eve 1661 resolution reflecting his internal conflict between leisure pursuits (plays and wine) and professional dedication
items.publicReaction: Not publicly known until diary publication; reveals character struggle familiar to modern readers

items.text: Here I drank wine upon necessity, being ill for the want of it.
items.context: Entry shortly after his New Year's oath, documenting his inability to maintain his resolution and justifying his lapse
items.publicReaction: Not public until diary publication; demonstrates Pepys' candid self-awareness about his weaknesses

items.text: But, Lord! how sad a sight it is to see the streets empty of people, and very few upon the ['Change].
items.context: During the Great Plague of 1665, observing the devastating emptiness of London's commercial center and the closure of shops
items.publicReaction: Not public until diary publication; later considered a vivid historical testament to the plague's impact on urban life

items.text: I have never lived so merrily (besides that I never got so much) as I have done this plague time.
items.context: Year-end reflection for 1665 documenting how his personal prosperity actually increased during the plague despite the tragedy affecting London
items.publicReaction: When published, struck readers as characteristically frank and somewhat callous, revealing Pepys' ambition and pleasure in his rising fortunes

items.text: In all things, in wisdom, courage, force and success, the Dutch have the best of us and do end the war with victory on their side.
items.context: Assessment during or after the Second Anglo-Dutch War (1665-1667), candidly evaluating England's naval defeat
items.publicReaction: When published, showed Pepys' professional realism about military outcomes; confirms historical records of English naval struggles

items.text: I down to the water-side, and there got a boat and through bridge, and there saw a lamentable fire.
items.context: Eyewitness account of the Great Fire of London beginning, documenting his journey from his home to observe the disaster
items.publicReaction: When published, recognized as one of the most important firsthand accounts of the Great Fire; widely cited by historians

items.text: it made me weep to see it.
items.context: Emotional response while witnessing the Great Fire of London from Bankside, observing the destruction of his city
items.publicReaction: When published, struck readers as genuine human emotion during catastrophe; often cited as evidence of Pepys' sensitivity

items.text: the fire running further, that in a very little time it got as far as the Steeleyard, while I was there.
items.context: Continuing eyewitness account of the Great Fire's rapid spread across London during the first day
items.publicReaction: Part of his detailed fire account; published version recognized as invaluable historical documentation of the fire's progression

items.text: Sir W. Pen and I did dig another, and put our wine in it; and I my Parmazan cheese, as well as my wine and some other things.
items.context: Describing his personal protective measures during the Great Fire, burying valuables in a pit to save them from the advancing flames
items.publicReaction: When published, provided readers with domestic details of how Londoners responded to the crisis

items.text: my wife, after the absence of her terms for seven weeks, gave me hopes of her being with child, but on the last day of the year she hath them again.
items.context: Intimate domestic observation about his wife's reproductive health, reflecting his hopes for an heir and subsequent disappointment
items.publicReaction: When published, readers found this candid medical detail characteristic of Pepys' detailed personal documentation

items.text: So home to supper, and to bed, it being my wedding night, but how many years I cannot tell; but my wife says ten.
items.context: Marking a wedding anniversary with characteristic Pepysian irony about his forgetfulness of the specific date
items.publicReaction: When published, struck readers as humorous self-deprecation and marital frankness
